text: Tissue characterization at an enhanced resolution across spatial omics platforms with deep generative model. Bohan Li, Feng Bao, Yimin Hou, Fengji Li, Hongjue Li, Yue Deng & Qionghai Dai. Nature Communications (2024) 15:6541. Abstract: Recent advances in spatial omics have expanded the spectrum of profiled molecular categories beyond transcriptomics. However, many of these technologies are constrained by limited spatial resolution, hindering our ability to deeply characterize intricate tissue architectures. Existing computational methods primarily focus on the resolution enhancement of transcriptomics data, lacking the adaptability to address the emerging spatial omics technologies that profile various omics types. Here, we introduce soScope, a unified generative framework designed to enhance data quality and spatial resolution for molecular profiles obtained from diverse spatial technologies. soScope aggregates multimodal tissue information from omics, spatial relations and images, and jointly infers omics profiles at enhanced resolutions with omics-specific modeling through distribution priors. With comprehensive evaluations on diverse spatial omics platforms, including Visium, Xenium, spatial-CUT&Tag, and slide-DNA/RNA-seq, soScope improves performances in identifying biologically meaningful intestine and kidney architectures, revealing embryonic heart structure that cannot be resolved at the original resolution and correcting sample and technical biases arising from sequencing and sample processing. Furthermore, soScope extends to spatial multiomics technology spatial-CITE-seq and spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, leveraging cross-omics reference for simultaneous multiomics enhancement. soScope provides a versatile tool to improve the utilization of continually expanding spatial omics technologies and resources.

Two challenges remain limiting the full potential of spatial omics: 1) Sample processing (freezing, formalin-fixation) can impact molecule states, reducing sequencing accuracy. 2) Most technologies use spatial barcodes with spot resolutions containing tens of cells, averaging readouts and limiting resolution. Computational methods to enhance resolution and reduce noise exist but predominantly focus on transcriptomics, using single modalities (spatial positions or images) and are not extendable to other omics types. Here we introduce soScope, a fully generative framework that models the generation process of spot-level profiles from diverse spatial omics technologies and aims to enhance their spatial resolution and data quality. soScope views each spot as the aggregation of 'subspots' at an enhanced spatial resolution, with their omics profiles associated with spatial positions and morphological patterns. soScope integrates spot omics profiles, spatial relations, and high-resolution morphological images using a multimodal deep learning framework and jointly infers omics profiles at the subspot resolution. By selecting omics-specific distributions, soScope allows for accurate modeling and variation reduction of different spatial omics data. soScope offers a unified tool that incorporates multimodal tissue profiles to enhance omics profiles with different molecular categories.

soScope Architecture: soScope integrates molecular profiles (X), spatial neighboring relations (A), and morphological image features (Y) to divide each spot into multiple subspots (X̂). The model includes three steps: 1) Spot-level representation learning: molecular profiles and spatial coordinates are embedded into latent representations (Z) with a graph encoder q(Z|X,A). 2) Subspot-level image feature learning: image patches corresponding to subspot regions are segmented and transformed into morphological features. 3) Subspot-level omics profile inference: spot representations and subspot image features are combined in an enhancement decoder p(X̂|Y,Z) to jointly infer subspot-level profiles, constrained by an omics-specific distribution and morphological similarities. The framework: Aims to divide expression profile x^(n) at the n^th spot s^(n) into K subspots s_1:K^(n): x^(n) = Σ_{k=1}^{K} x̂_k^(n) + e^(n), n=1...N, where x̂_1:K^(n) are latent expressions, e^(n) is Gaussian noise. Probabilistic generative model: z^(n) ∼ Gaussian(0, σ^2 I); ω_k^(n) = f(y_k^(n), z^(n)), k=1...K; x̂_k^(n) | z^(n), y_k^(n) ∼ P(ω_k^(n)), k=1...K; x^(n) | x̂_1:K^(n) ∼ Gaussian( Σ_k x̂_k^(n), σ^2 I ). f(·) is a neural network; P(·) is an omics-specific distribution. Log-likelihood: log p(X|Y) = log{ Σ_{X̂} Σ_Z p(X|X̂) p(X̂|Y, Z) p(Z) }. Optimization: Solved via variational inference. Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO): ELBO = E_{q(Z|X,A)}{ E_{p(X̂|Y,Z)}[ log p(X|X̂) ] } - D_KL[ q(Z|X,A) || p(Z) ]. Image regularization term L_Image = CE(Λ, W) ensures consistency between omics and image similarity matrices. Overall objective: L = ELBO - β L_Image. After optimization, enhanced profiles: Z* = E_{q(Z|X,A)}[Z]; X̂* = E_{p(X̂|Z*,Y)}[X̂].

section: Evaluation on Spatial Transcriptomics Datasets (Visium, Xenium)
text: Benchmarking on Spatial Transcriptomics: Compared soScope with iStar, XFuse, BayesSpace, and an image-only soScope variant. Used simulated 'low-resolution' data by merging neighboring spots. Human Intestine (Visium): 2,649 spots, 9 region markers. soScope achieved the highest consistency (Pearson correlation) and lowest reconstruction error (MSE) compared to others. KS distance evaluation showed soScope and iStar yielded the most pronounced separations for most genes. Mouse Head (Xenium): 2,619 spots (cell-level), 379 genes. soScope and iStar performed well, but soScope showed more stable performance across genes of different variability or abundance. soScope best preserved gene correlation patterns. Mouse Kidney (Xenium, Single-Cell Resolution): Task: recover cellular expressions from aggregated square regions. soScope outperformed iStar, which tended to over-estimate in high-density regions. soScope demonstrated more consistent expression with ground truth. Impact of Image Input: Test on human ductal carcinoma (Xenium). soScope without image input underperformed on genes highly correlated with image features, but performed similarly for lowly correlated genes, showing image input provides useful guidance when morphology correlates with biology.

section: Evaluation on Spatial-CUT&Tag and Multi-Scale Stress Test
text: soScope on Spatial Chromatin Accessibility (spatial-CUT&Tag): Applied to mouse embryo (E11) with 1974 spots, top 60 variable peaks from four organs. Used Poisson distribution for peak counts. Compared to spatial-based (Spatial linear, Spatial GP), image-based (Image linear, Image GP, Image MLP), and joint (Joint MLP) methods. Simulation: soScope achieved the highest correlation and lowest MSE. Direct Enhancement: soScope effectively suppressed out-of-region sporadic expressions (noise) and correctly identified expressed regions, while other methods were misled by noise or failed. Revealed Embryonic Heart Structure: Original low-resolution data could not resolve two subregions (trabecular vs. compact ventricular myocardium). soScope's enhanced profiles revealed distinct spatial activities for marker genes (Fhl2, Ldha), validated against a human embryonic heart ST dataset. Multi-Scale Stress Test on Slide-DNA/RNA-seq: Used mouse liver tumor data (slide-DNA-seq, slide-RNA-seq) to test robustness across decreasing resolutions (from 60x60 down to 10x10). Task: Recover top 2 DNA Principal Components (PCs) or gene expressions. soScope consistently showed stable performance with smallest accuracy decrease. Only soScope and Spatial GP could capture a detailed substructure (k=4 clustering) across all resolutions. soScope accurately reconstructed clone regions despite slight image-DNA coordinate mismatch, unlike image-based methods.

section: Extension to Spatial Multiomics (spatial-CITE-seq, spatial ATAC-RNA-seq)
text: Multiomics soScope: Modified to simultaneously enhance multiple omics. 1) Spatial graph constructed from multiomics similarities. 2) Graph encoder uses multiomics input for joint latent representations. 3) Decoder outputs parameters for all enhanced profiles simultaneously. Distributions: For spatial-CITE-seq, NB for transcripts, Poisson for proteins. For spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, NB for transcripts, Gaussian for normalized ATAC peaks. Application to spatial-CITE-seq (Human Skin): 1,618 spots, 283 proteins, 114 variable genes. Original transcript data had high noise; protein data showed clearer spatial patterns. Multi-soScope leveraged protein data to guide transcript enhancement, resulting in tissue structures with improved spatial coherence (reduced within-cluster distances). It effectively suppressed technical noise (e.g., random noise for FADS1, grid pattern for RNA18S5) in transcripts. Enhanced protein profiles also refined boundaries. Consistency between region-matched transcripts and proteins increased after enhancement, with multi-soScope achieving the best performance. Application to spatial ATAC-RNA-seq (Mouse Embryo E13): 1,874 spots, 25 variable genes and their peaks. Multi-soScope improved spatial enrichment of gene expressions, suppressed sporadic noise, and significantly improved correlation between genes and their corresponding ATAC peaks after enhancement.

section: Methods Details: Datasets and Experiment Setup
text: Datasets: Human intestine (Visium, GSE158328). Mouse head & kidney (Xenium, 10x Genomics). Human ductal carcinoma (Xenium, 10x Genomics). Mouse embryo E11 (spatial-CUT&Tag, GSE165217). Human embryonic heart (ST, Mendeley). Mouse liver tumor (slide-DNA/RNA-seq, PRJNA768453/SCP1278). Human skin (spatial-CITE-seq, GSE213264). Mouse embryo E13 (spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, UCSC). In silico 'Low-Resolution' Simulation: For benchmarking, merged neighboring spots to create low-resolution input. Visium: aggregated 7 spots in a hexagon. Xenium: aggregated cells in squared regions. Other platforms: merged spots in square regions (e.g., 2x2 for spatial-CUT&Tag). Multi-scale test: varied region size from 2x2 to 6x6. Subspot Image Features: For each subspot, segmented corresponding H&E/bright-field image region, resized to 299x299, fed into pretrained Inception-v3 to obtain 2048-dim features. Omics-Specific Distributions: Transcripts: Negative Binomial (NB). Protein / Histone modification: Poisson. DNA PCs / Normalized ATAC peaks: Gaussian. (See Supplementary Table 1).

section: Methods: Compared Approaches and Evaluation Metrics
text: Compared Approaches: Spatial-based: BayesSpace (v1.2.1), Spatial linear (nearest neighbor avg), Spatial GP (Gaussian Process on coordinates). Image-based: iStar, XFuse, Image linear, Image GP, Image MLP. Joint: Joint MLP (concatenated image+position features). soScope Implementation: Python with PyTorch and PyG. Evaluation Metrics: Recovery Accuracy: Pearson correlation, Mean Square Error (MSE). Differential Expression: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) distance. Gene Variability: Biological variability from scran::modelGeneVar. Gene Abundance: Total expression counts. Cluster Compactness: Average spatial distance between spots within a cluster. Subpopulation Identification: Louvain algorithm on top PCs. Statistical Tests: Two-sided t-tests, two-sided rank-sum test. Software & Code: soScope is available on GitHub: https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/soScope and Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.12740738.

Discussion: soScope is a generative framework that models data generation and integrates omics, spatial relations, and images for resolution enhancement. It uses omics-specific distributions (NB, Poisson, Gaussian). Evaluated across diverse platforms (Visium, Xenium, spatial-CUT&Tag, slide-DNA/RNA-seq, spatial-CITE-seq, spatial ATAC-RNA-seq) and tissue types. Demonstrated capabilities: refines tissue domain identifications, improves marker distinguishability, corrects data/technical bias, reveals substructures (e.g., heart layers), and enables simultaneous multiomics enhancement. Limitations & Future Work: Current soScope provides enhanced profiles for pre-designated subspots, not subcellular resolution. Could be modified to incorporate paired single-cell omics data for higher resolution. Can incorporate human expert labels for semi-supervised learning. For large multi-section datasets, can be trained on a subset and applied to the rest. Conclusion: soScope is a versatile tool to enhance data quality and spatial resolution across diverse spatial omics technologies, helping to fully leverage expanding spatial omics resources for understanding complex tissue structures.
